explicit_text: I was born in New York City in 1919 and went to school there. I left New York City at the beginning of World War II to join the U.S. Navy. After the war, I returned briefly before moving to California, where I've lived ever since. Growing up in New York, I was exposed to art through institutions like the Brooklyn Museum and the Metropolitan Museum of Art, and I was influenced by the cultural environment of the city.

explicit_text: I attended Brooklyn College and graduated in 1939 with a major in psychology and a minor in education. While there, I took regular art courses which sparked my interest in art. Brooklyn College was where I began to seriously engage with art, even though I couldn't afford to buy much at the time.

explicit_text: Around the late 1930s, the Associated American Artists were dealers in New York who published and sold affordable graphics for the masses. They featured artists like Edward Hopper and the Soyer brothers, and their lithographs appealed to me, though I couldn't afford them as a young person. Their program was important in making art accessible during tough economic times.

His work was popular at the time and caught my attention as a young art student in New York. Although I couldn't afford to buy his prints, his art was influential in the cultural scene I was part of.

explicit_text: In 1936, the New York Post offered reproductions of Van Gogh paintings through a coupon program. I remember collecting those coupons as a kid, hoping to get one of his prints. Though my family was poor and art was a luxury, Van Gogh's work was one of the few accessible ways I could connect with great art during that time.

explicit_text: In 1938, Adolf Hitler was busy selling great paintings out of the German museums in Bern. This was a significant event in the art world during my youth, reflecting the turbulent political times. It was something I was aware of as a young art enthusiast in New York.
